caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
a: Plants range from older batch systems to highly automated drum plants, so exposure varies materially by installed controls.
rho: Computer control is evidence of digital feasibility, not AI labor substitution.
rho: Seasonality requires multi-season evidence to avoid weather and project-mix confounding.
e: The provided count is an ESTIMATE based on a margin bridge and may include vertically integrated or product-only businesses outside the lens.
e: Product sales with embedded specification responsibility sit near the service-lens boundary.

s5: Plant and operating-company ownership can change separately, complicating control-transfer classification.
q: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.

o: Higher automation can reduce labor per ton without reducing the share of demand requiring accountable plant operation.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Predominantly per-ton or per-load sales of specified mixtures, with repeat project and account relationships; qualifying toll or contract mixing earns processing revenue for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Paving contractors classified elsewhere, asphalt roofing manufacturers, captive plants producing solely for an affiliated contractor, commodity distributors, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market asphalt-mixture and paving-block manufacturers with repeat external customers, including independent hot-, warm-, and cold-mix plants and genuine contract or toll-mixing operations serving road contractors, governments, utilities, and commercial pavers.

a.bridge: EPA process descriptions and BLS broader-subsector occupations describe plant operations rather than a wage-weighted AI task audit for NAICS 324121. The range maps exposure to order intake, quoting, dispatch, mix scheduling, quality-data review, moisture and energy optimization, predictive maintenance, purchasing, compliance reporting, and customer communication, while assigning low substitution to loader, plant, laboratory, maintenance, and field-verification work; broader NAICS 324 data may overweight petroleum-refinery roles.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
Plants range from older batch systems to highly automated drum plants, so exposure varies materially by installed controls.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted two-week task study across at least twenty independent asphalt plants, split among loader, control room, laboratory, maintenance, dispatch, sales, estimating, environmental, administration, and management, with current automation mapped.
a.rationale: Modern asphalt batching is computer-controlled and produces extensive mix, scale, temperature, moisture, energy, and maintenance data, creating AI opportunities in scheduling, quality prediction, diagnostics, and administration. Physical aggregate handling, plant maintenance, sampling, loadout, environmental control, and accountable release of customer-specific mixes remain operator-intensive.

d5.bridge: Industry recycling and warm-mix production data proxy active asphalt output and technology adoption rather than a direct five-year constant-price demand forecast for eligible LMM toll processors. Infrastructure funding and road maintenance support aggregate demand, but public budgets, construction cycles, vertical integration, regional population, and mix durability can move eligible-operator quantity differently from national tonnage.
caveats: Recycled-material tonnage is an input measure, not total end-demand or LMM eligible-firm output.
The 2026 expiration profile of current federal authorization and future appropriations create forecast uncertainty.
d5.change_evidence: State-by-state five-year programmed highway and local-road tonnage, letting schedules, pavement-condition backlog, private construction forecasts, plant utilization, and independent-versus-captive sourcing for each candidate service area.
d5.rationale: Roads, parking areas, airports, and utility cuts require recurring resurfacing and repair, while asphalt is locally produced because hot material cannot travel indefinitely. NAPA reports 101.4 million tons of reclaimed pavement recycled into new mixtures in 2024 and warm-mix technologies at 40.2% of the estimated market, evidence of a large active production base. The base assumes modest real quantity growth with cyclical downside.

caveats: The provided count is an ESTIMATE based on a margin bridge and may include vertically integrated or product-only businesses outside the lens.
Product sales with embedded specification responsibility sit near the service-lens boundary.
e.change_evidence: A contract and ownership screen of the 275 estimated firms identifying external versus affiliate sales, toll-processing fees, repeat-account share, normalized EBITDA, plant ownership, environmental permits and liabilities, equipment age, and customer concentration.
e.rationale: The frozen lens requires an outsourced service, while most establishments manufacture and sell asphalt mixture as a product. Only genuine toll or contract mixing, or product-plus-accountability arrangements that economically function as recurring outsourced processing, clearly qualify. Eligibility is further reduced by vertical integration with paving contractors, captive production, environmental liabilities, owner dependence, and equipment condition.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 324121 ($1,632,773,797) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 324121 ($18,641,212,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $1,112,576 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 324121); class <5: 135 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,116,748 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $456,604 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,530,393 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,249,703 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 47 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,173,404 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,076,404 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,047,161 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,255,409 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $46,966,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,880,519 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $201,224,509 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,479,391 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 275

caveats: The operator may be a vertically integrated paving company rather than a standalone eligible firm.
Higher automation can reduce labor per ton without reducing the share of demand requiring accountable plant operation.
o.change_evidence: Operating and regulatory data from highly automated asphalt plants covering minimum staffing, remote-operation scope, unattended hours, quality-release authority, environmental compliance, maintenance staffing, and customer self-service.
o.rationale: Any asphalt quantity still demanded requires an accountable physical plant operator to receive and test materials, manage recipes and temperatures, operate emissions controls, maintain equipment, load trucks, meet approved specifications, and coordinate time-sensitive delivery. Software can reduce staffing and automate transactions, but cannot supply heated mixture without controlled local production assets and responsibility for quality and permits.

caveats: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
Retention depends heavily on local plant density, permitted capacity, aggregate access, and whether the customer is an affiliate.
q.change_evidence: Matched customer-level bid, invoice, haul-distance, queue-time, and gross-margin data before and after automation across public and private work, including binder escalators, rebid timing, competitor openings, and lost bids for at least three seasons.
q.rationale: Per-ton bids and transparent binder and aggregate costs expose savings to customer negotiation, especially on public work. Yet hot mix must reach the job at workable temperature, restricting the practical delivery radius and reducing distant competition; reliability, queue time, specification compliance, and scarce peak-season capacity can preserve a meaningful share of operational gains.

caveats: Computer control is evidence of digital feasibility, not AI labor substitution.
Seasonality requires multi-season evidence to avoid weather and project-mix confounding.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after results from AI-enabled dispatch, moisture compensation, burner optimization, predictive maintenance, and quality-control deployments at comparable plants, reporting labor hours, fuel use, rejected mix, downtime, throughput, and operator overrides for two full seasons.
rho.rationale: Digital order, scale, recipe, temperature, fuel, and maintenance data make planning and decision-support use cases implementable within five years. Realization is constrained by seasonal peaks, fragmented legacy controls, sensor quality, mix-design approvals, state specifications, environmental permits, cyber risk, scarce integration talent, and the high cost of off-spec loads or plant downtime.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Discounting intentions for closures, family/internal transfers, failed sales, permit-transfer friction, remediation liabilities, and buyer financing yields the base; an older fragmented plant population could make the all-industry proxy conservative, while vertical-integration sales may not leave an eligible standalone firm.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures plans rather than consummated transfers.
Plant and operating-company ownership can change separately, complicating control-transfer classification.
s5.change_evidence: Ten years of closed asphalt-plant transfers, closures, and permit changes from state air agencies, industry brokers, PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, SBA lenders, and county property records, matched to owner age and lens eligibility.
s5.rationale: A local, fragmented plant base and succession needs should produce transfers, but environmental diligence, zoning, heavy equipment, aggregate and binder supply, customer ties, and captive contractor relationships complicate going-concern sales. Some exits will be plant closures or acquisitions by integrated paving companies rather than qualifying independent control transfers.

principal_driver: Local, time-sensitive production and repeat infrastructure demand make throughput, quality, and dispatch improvements operationally valuable.
principal_weakness: Most establishments are product manufacturers or captive plants, leaving only a small and unobserved share eligible as recurring outsourced-service firms.

run_meta.title: Asphalt Paving Mixture and Block Manufacturing

sources.supports: Describes batch, continuous, parallel-flow, and counterflow hot-mix production; states aggregate and reclaimed asphalt exceed 92% of mixture weight and details metering, drying, screening, weighing, mixing, and operator-controlled production.
sources.title: AP-42 Section 11.1: Hot Mix Asphalt Plants

sources.supports: Describes hot-mix plants as historically numerous and often small, notes many firms had one to three plants, and explains that hot delivery limits plants' operating areas.
sources.title: Atmospheric Emissions from the Asphalt Industry

sources.supports: Documents a batch plant where customer-requested mix details and total tonnage are programmed into a control-room computer, which divides loads into batches and controls weighed components.
sources.title: Hot Mix Asphalt Plants: Truck Loading and Instrumental Methods Testing, Plant D

sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% had no plan.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports more than 96% of reclaimed asphalt mixture reused, warm-mix technologies at 40.2% of estimated 2024 market, and 101.4 million tons of reclaimed pavement recycled into new mixtures.
sources.title: 15th Annual Asphalt Pavement Industry Survey on Recycled Materials and Warm-Mix Asphalt Usage: 2024 Construction Season

title: Asphalt Paving Mixture and Block Manufacturing
